title: Course of Law - Criminal Procedure in India
description: Complete step-by-step procedure from FIR to final judgment in criminal cases under Criminal Procedure Code and new Bharatiya Suraksha Sanhita 2023
source: Criminal Procedure Code 1973 & Bharatiya Suraksha Sanhita 2023

phases.description: When crime is committed, any person can give information to police. Police registers FIR for cognizable offence.
phases.timeFrame: Immediate (within 24 hours of crime being reported)
steps: Person reports crime to police station (orally or written)
Police records information in FIR Register
FIR is given unique number and registered
Informant/victim gets copy of FIR free of charge
FIR details recorded: crime date, time, location, persons involved, nature of offence
rights: Right to register FIR for any cognizable crime
Right to get free copy of FIR
Right to remain anonymous if fear exists

phases.description: Police investigates the crime, collects evidence, records statements, and identifies accused.
phases.timeFrame: Usually 90 days (can extend to 180 days with magistrate approval)
steps: Police visits crime scene, collects physical evidence
Police records statements of victim and witnesses
Police searches for accused person
Police seizes relevant documents and exhibits
Post-mortem conducted if death involved
Forensic/medical examination conducted
Police files inquest report (if death) or investigation report
Police submits investigation to higher officer for verification
rights: Right to privacy of victim
Right to protection during investigation
Right to have access to investigation reports (through court)

phases.description: When sufficient evidence of crime is found, police can arrest the accused person.
phases.timeFrame: During or after investigation, anytime before chargesheet filed
steps: Police identifies and arrests suspect
Police informs accused of grounds for arrest
Arrest is recorded in station diary with name, time, date
Accused is given right to inform family member
Accused is given right to consult with lawyer
Personal search conducted (video recorded)
Accused produced for medical examination if needed
Mugshots and fingerprints taken
rights: Right to know grounds of arrest
Right to remain silent (cannot be forced to confess)
Right to consult lawyer of choice
Right to inform family member about arrest
Right to medical examination
Protection against torture and abuse
Right to get copy of FIR

phases.description: Arrested person must be produced before magistrate within 24 hours. Magistrate verifies legality of arrest.
phases.timeFrame: Within 24 hours of arrest (strict rule)
steps: Arrested person produced before magistrate
Magistrate verifies identity and arrest legality
Police presents case before magistrate
Victim or witness can give statement
Accused gets right to speak in own defense
Magistrate decides whether to remand or grant bail
rights: Right to know charges against you
Right to speak in own defense
Right to cross-examine prosecution witness
Right to apply for bail
Right to legal representation

phases.description: Magistrate decides if accused should be released on bail or kept in police/judicial custody.
phases.timeFrame: Decided at first production or within 15 days
steps: Bail: If bailable offence, accused has right to bail. Police must release on bail.
Remand (Police): If needed for further investigation, accused remanded to police custody for 3-7 days
Remand (Judicial): Accused held in judicial custody in jail for investigation continuation
Bail conditions: Accused may be released on bond and bail amount

rightToBail.bailable: For bailable offences, police must release on bail (right to bail)
rightToBail.nonBailable: For non-bailable offences, bail is discretionary with magistrate

bailTypes.bailConditions: Usually depositing security, not leaving jurisdiction without court permission, not tampering with evidence

remandTypes: Police Remand: Custody with police, maximum 15 days total, maximum 7 days at a time
Judicial Remand: Custody in jail, can be extended as long as investigation continuing
rights: Right to bail if bailable offence
Right to apply for bail multiple times
Right to know bail conditions
Right to protect personal liberty
Right to challenge remand in higher court

phases.description: If accused remanded, police continues investigation. Interrogation of accused happens.
phases.timeFrame: Maximum 15 days police remand, can extend judicial remand
steps: Accused questioned in custody (interrogation)
Confession (if given) recorded properly with magistrate
Scene visits with accused if needed
Evidence linked to accused
Chargesheet preparation starts
Accused produced again before magistrate (every 3 days for bail/remand extension)
interrogationRights: Right to silence - cannot be forced to speak
Right to have lawyer present during questioning (if demanded)
Confession to police not admissible in court unless confirmed before magistrate

phases.description: Police submits detailed chargesheet with evidence showing they have case against accused.
phases.timeFrame: Within 60-90 days for regular cases (extendable with magistrate permission)
steps: Police prepares chargesheet with all evidence and witness details
Chargesheet lists all sections of law violated
Chargesheet mentions bail status of accused
Copy given to accused free of charge
Chargesheet filed with magistrate
Case transferred to court for further proceeding
chargesheetContents: Police opinion on sufficiency of evidence
rights: Right to get free copy of chargesheet
Right to challenge chargesheet claim in court
Right to file counter statement
Right to additional investigation petition

phases.description: Court reads chargesheet. If sufficient evidence, court frames charges. If not, accused discharged.
phases.timeFrame: 90 days after chargesheet (can extend with good reason)
steps: Reading of chargesheet before magistrate/sessions court
Accused given copy of chargesheet and list of witnesses/documents
Accused hears evidence summary and prosecution arguments
Magistrate decides if sufficient evidence for trial
If YES: Framing of charges (list of offences accused will be tried for)
If NO: Discharge of accused (case ends, accused is free)

chargingProcess.framingCharges: Court frames charges specifying sections accused violated and facts to be proved
chargingProcess.accused: Accused asked whether they plead guilty or not guilty

rights: Right to be heard before charges framed
Right to challenge chargesheet
Right to seek additional time to meet evidence
Right to examine evidence before trial

phases.description: Prosecution presents evidence to prove guilt. Witnesses examined by prosecutor.
phases.timeFrame: Weeks to months depending on case complexity
steps: Prosecution presents witnesses one by one
Witness gives evidence under oath before court
Prosecutor questions witness (examination-in-chief)
Accused or defense lawyer cross-examines witness
Prosecutor can re-examine if needed (limited scope)
Documents and exhibits presented and authenticated
Physical evidence shown (weapons, stolen items, etc.)
Forensic evidence presented by experts
rights: Right to cross-examine prosecution witnesses
Right to challenge evidence
Right to legal representation during prosecution case
Right to remain silent

phases.description: Accused presents defense evidence. Accused can present witnesses and evidence favoring innocence.
phases.timeFrame: After prosecution completes its case
steps: Accused has right to present defense
Accused can present own witnesses
Accused can testify in own behalf (optional)
Defense witnesses examined and cross-examined
Defense documents and evidence presented
Defense arguments presented before court
defenseEvidence: Alibi evidence (proof of being elsewhere when crime occurred)
rights: Right to present defense evidence
Right to testify or remain silent (accused's choice)
Right to cross-examine prosecution witnesses
Right to legal representation
Right to call witnesses (can be compelled by court)

phases.description: Both sides present final arguments. Prosecutor and defense lawyer argue case before judge.
phases.timeFrame: After all evidence presented
steps: Prosecution presents closing arguments (what evidence proves guilt)
Defense presents closing arguments (why evidence doesn't prove guilt)
Both sides can cite legal precedents
Both sides address judge's questions
Jury argument (if jury trial, which is rare in India)
Judge reserves judgment or takes time to decide
arguments: Prosecution argues how evidence proves guilt beyond reasonable doubt
Defense argues how evidence leaves reasonable doubt about guilt
rights: Right to legal representation during arguments
Right to present oral arguments
Right to challenge prosecution theory

phases.description: Judge pronounces verdict - either acquitting (not guilty) or convicting (guilty) the accused.
phases.timeFrame: Judge decides based on evidence presented
steps: Judge analyzes all evidence
Judge considers burden of proof (guilt beyond reasonable doubt)
Judge checks if each element of offence is proved
Judge pronounces verdict: GUILTY or NOT GUILTY
Judge gives written judgment with detailed reasoning
Judgment must follow law and precedents

verdictTypes.acquittal: Not guilty - Accused is set free, charges dismissed
verdictTypes.conviction: Guilty - Pronounced culpable. Proceeds to sentencing.

phases.burdenOfProof: Prosecution must prove guilt BEYOND REASONABLE DOUBT. Mere suspicion is insufficient. If any reasonable doubt, must acquit.
rights: Right to reasoned judgment
Right to know why verdictgiven
Right to appeal verdict
Right to bail pending appeal

phases.description: Judge decides punishment - either fine, imprisonment, or both, or other sentence.
phases.timeFrame: Immediately after or within few days of conviction
steps: Judge considers severity of crime
Judge considers criminal history of convict
Judge hears victim impact statement
Judge considers convict's background and circumstances
Judge considers sentencing guidelines and precedents
Judge awards sentence: Fine, Imprisonment, or Both
sentenceTypes: Life Imprisonment: Till natural death (parole possible)
Death Penalty: For rarest of rare cases (e.g., murder)
Probation: Release under supervision for minor offences
Suspended Sentence: Release with threat of jail if reoffend
rights: Right to know reason for sentence
Right to appeal sentence
Right to mercy petition
Right to parole/remission after serving time

phases.description: Convicted person can appeal to higher court to challenge verdict or sentence.
phases.timeFrame: Within 90 days of judgment, appeal can be filed
steps: Convict (through lawyer) files appeal petition in high court
Appeal states grounds for challenging conviction/sentence
Copy of judgment and entire case file sent to high court
Prosecution files counter-submission
High court reads entire record
High court hearing scheduled
Convict and prosecution arguments heard
High court decision: Confirm conviction or set aside
appealGrounds: Verdict is against evidence or law
rights: Right to free legal aid on appeal
Right to bail pending appeal (in some cases)
Right to be heard in appeal
Right to further appeal to Supreme Court

phases.description: Final appeal to Supreme Court against high court judgment (only on important legal questions).
phases.timeFrame: Within 30 days of high court judgment (special leave petition)
steps: Convict files petition for special leave to appeal in Supreme Court
Supreme Court reviews if case raises important legal question
If permitted, Supreme Court hears the case
Supreme Court examines entire record and hearing arguments
Supreme Court pronounces final verdict
rights: Right to petition Supreme Court
Right to be heard in Supreme Court
Right to free legal aid

phases.description: Convict serves sentence. Can apply for parole or remission.
phases.timeFrame: Duration of sentence (years or life)
steps: Convict sent to jail for imprisonment
Undertaking jail discipline
Studying behavior record for parole eligibility
Parole application after serving minimum 50% sentence
State parole authority reviews application
Release on parole with conditions or life imprisonment sentence reduction after certain years
paroleRights: Right to apply for parole after serving minimum time
Right to remission for good behavior
Right to medical care in jail

civilProcedure.title: Civil Procedure - Dispute Resolution
civilProcedure.summary: In civil cases (non-criminal), disputes between parties are resolved through courts. Burden of proof is 'preponderance of probabilities' (more likely than not), NOT beyond reasonable doubt.

phases.timeFrame: Before statute of limitations expires
phases.description: Plaintiff files suit against defendant in appropriate court

phases.timeFrame: Within statutory periods
phases.description: Both parties file written statements and arguments

phases.timeFrame: Before trial
phases.description: Both sides exchange evidence and documents

phases.timeFrame: Months to years
phases.description: Case settles or judge passes judgment based on evidence

phases.timeFrame: Within 90-120 days of judgment
phases.description: Losing party can appeal to higher court
